title: Please share your favourite research papers on AI in trading.
author: Traditional_Ear5237
selftext:
Hey, over the course of the last 3 years I have read over 50 different research papers where AI is used to predict the market or manage positions or both. All of the papers I thought have good standing we rebuilt the code for and replicated the data and tested. And guess what? Almost all of them are complete BS. The most common one is predicting the next hour or next minute, whatever the timeframe, and then plotting results. And when you plot it looks like the AI is predicting like a god. But that is a trick of sight, when you look at individual predictions, losses versus gains they are spread out 50/50 meaning there is nothing to it.

There are a couple of other common themes but nothing substantial. The ones on RL are decent though, some of them. So I wonder if you guys have read any good research papers and you can share with me here. Eager to learn more and try more of them on real trading.

comments.author: Far-Photograph-2342
comments.body: Yeah, most of these papers look good on charts but fall apart in real testing, and the only somewhat useful ones are usually RL or execution-focused, not price prediction.

replies.author: Traditional_Ear5237
replies.body: RL are my favourite, honest opinion is that RL is the way to 'solve the market'. Not LLMs, not ML.

comments.author: No-Consequence-1779
comments.body:
Anything using the word ‘predict’ is false. 
At best, you measure things to product signals. 
Then it’s your rules to guess. 

The first issue with using ‘AI’, is first, it is an LLM ; not actual intelligence. Not even close. Anyone calling it the generic term does not possess the basic understanding of what they are talking about. 

The second is with using LLMs is they are non deterministic. Same input, different output. They are designed this way. Changing temperature can alter this behavior. 

The third and most profoundly ignorant issue is, it ultimately comes down to mathematics, like always, to measure and generate signals. LLMs are absolutely not required. They never have been. Successful traders and there are only a few are not playing around with it. 

It is ultimately a lazy way to try to assign actual thinking to something else, probably because the person can not grasp the simple math involved. And it’s basic math. Hold crap. 

For marketing- same issue - mislead people of questionable intelligence. 

The interesting part is, if you understand enough to know what to measure to produce signals that may be correct more than not; you would need to build it to test it.

 If it works, then you think, “hmmmm. Maybe I can screw this up by having an LLM involved ”.  Maybe send the time value series data to have the LLM try to calculate it - just to add latency and risk. Yes. This is a good idea.

replies.author: No-Consequence-1779
replies.body:
The common data points to track are, but not limited to: volatility , order book (depth: primary, secondary), volume, imbalance…  just a few. These produce some numbers, then reduced to a buy sell hold signal with a confidence.  (For real time trading). 

Plus tracking others to see if the market is dumping or not.  You’ve probably seen by now, there is only so much cash - it moves around. Equities to crypto to just cash. 

Events play a large part. Geopolitical, then the standard reports like earnings and forecasts. Then supply chain issues, energy, ….  

Though just watching the numbers is what matters for realtime trading.

replies.author: Traditional_Ear5237
replies.body: agreed, for higher frequency day trading the geopolitics dont matter much except for a few days per month. The lower frequency you go the more it is momentum and volume movements. The higher timeframe you go, it is the opposite, more geopolitical and more fundamental.

replies.author: No-Consequence-1779
replies.body: It’s also much easier working with 500k plus.  Starts to pile up. The invest when young first job is so incredibly important. Most these ‘traders’ play around and end up broke.
